text: The surgeon mobilizes the right colon to access the retroperitoneum, tracing the ureter proximally to the renal hilum. The superior vein draining the tumor is clipped and divided, followed by clipping and dividing the main stem and branches of the superior artery supplying the tumor.

The surgeon uses intravenous indocyanine green and fluorescence imaging to confirm no blood flow to the tumor while maintaining excellent blood flow to the normal kidney tissue. Intraoperative ultrasound is employed to delineate the tumor margins. Ischemia is induced by clamping the inferior artery and vein, followed by sharp dissection to excise the tumor.

The surgeon completes the inner layer of renography by oversewing the tumor bed. Early unclamping is performed, resulting in a total warm ischemia time of eleven minutes. The outer layer of renography is finished using interrupted sutures and a sliding clip technique.
The surgeon completes final hemostasis by applying flow seal and tail seal. The excised specimen is placed in an endocatch bag for removal, and a GP drain is inserted into the surgical site.
